speaker: interviewee
text: Hide your tummy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's one thing. I don't like to take pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gerda Kahan, tape five. Could you please introduce this handsome gentleman who gave you those beautiful flowers?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband, David Kahan. D-A-V-I-D K-A-H-A-N. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell us what it's been like to be married how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our marriage has--

speaker: interviewee
text: Has its ups and downs.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, our marriage is-- was and is like a beautiful dream, [BACKROUND NOISE] which I hope will never end. And-- and it's ideal. That's the only way I can describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were there things that you had to work out to be able to, you know, put your past behind you and to have a happy married life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Because we both went through the same hell. So he understood when I dreamt at night, and I understood when he dreams at night. And when he's upset or moody, I can cope with it, because I have the understanding. I suppose that's why we lasted that long [LAUGHS] together. But I'm sure it wasn't easy for him either, because he has no family and you have to cope with it. So we have each other to compensate for it, and thank God, our children feel the same way, that we are one unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the fact that we were both in a concentration camp helped us achieve a unified position with regard to religion after the war. Because we were both--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told this stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: --not religious. And we came to the conclusion-- when life became a little more normal, and Gerda was expecting her first child, and we came to the conclusion that one has to have something to rely-- to-- to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold onto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold onto. And children who are brought up in a religious spirit have a much fuller life at the end. And that's why we decided-- we both decided that we'll become religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you get a sense in the-- in the camps that people who were religious had a-- had a easier time, a harder time? What would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was nothing. No rule or regulation for it, because you have a lot of people that have never been religious and turned out to be very religious after the war, or the other way around. I know people that have been brought up in orthodox homes in a very pious environment, and they have turned away completely. Because as I said before, you want to call God to [NON-ENGLISH] and everybody expresses it in a different way. And you get different answers. You have different needs, right? So there is-- there is no rule or regulation for it. Everybody came out-- I personally think that I came out as a better person. I have more understanding for others.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see somebody drunk, I will say there's a reason for it. I mellowed. I-- I have seen so much horror, disgrace, by-- by-- even by Jewish men towards other women in the camp. I mean, he gave her a blanket because she wants to be his girlfriend, right? He was the shoemaker or the-- the tailor You understand? And then that blanket was quickly taken away because she was shipped someplace else. He didn't need her. So I have much more understanding for people, and I will never condemn anybody for anything they are doing. There is a reason for everything. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Leo Gross, taken in Beelitz in 1938. He perished in Auschwitz in 1944, in November, after spending three and a half years in the concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my mother, Julia Gross. She survived the concentration camp, and she died in November, 1975, in New York. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my brother, David Gross, who disappeared in Poland sometimes between 1942 and 1944, and has never been seen since. [? His ?] was known to everybody as [? Arvid. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the front of a sketch book that my brother used in 1940. And I found it after the war. This is the front page, the cover. I found it after the war by a Polish child playing with it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one of his drawings that was in the book, [BACKROUND NOISE] which was in 1942, just before we were sent to a concentration camp, and unfortunately, he never came back. Isn't it a disgrace and a shame that such a talent was killed? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture in 1934, just before we had left Germany. And this is how my childhood ended, right then and there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the center of this picture is my mother, shortly after she had an operation in 1941. And the Germans wouldn't permit her to stay in the hospital, and they sent her out with a blood clot in her leg. On the right side is myself. On the left is a cousin in whose house we have stayed in [? Vialichka. ?] And the poor girl wanted so much to escape, and she had Christian papers and made it from [? Vialichka ?] to Krakow. At the railroad station, somebody recognized and went over to the Germans and said, this is a Jew, and she's running away. And they took her and killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hella ?] [? Sobel. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my husband. This is the way he looked when we met and when we married. Unfortunately, time doesn't stand still, and we both have changed and got old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just, you know, go from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Either way. Just go from-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: The standing row or the sitting row.

speaker: interviewee
text: The back row, yeah. This is my family. Those are my millions. From left to right is my grandson, Stevie Stein, Michelle Stein, my son-in-law, Artie Stein, and my daughter, Lillian. In the center is my daughter, Judy, the youngest one, and her husband, Lawrence. To the right you have my daughter, Terry, and her husband, Kenny. In front of it is my grandson, Danny. In the front row is my grandson Robby. He's the oldest grandson. My husband, my youngest grandson, Andrew Scharf, and in the center is Julie Turkel, myself, and Jay Scharf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Julie's bat mitzvah.
